summary: Small atypical personal/identity register, provenance unknown, held at MNAAHP Lima. 32 cords, 0-SEUL flat depth L1. v3 unlocks 8 vocabulary occurrences (v1: 0). KEY SIGNATURE: chiki x6 (reflexive 'self/same') on 6 consecutive W cords p14-p18+p21, plus chiqa x1 (verification) and tama x1 (group/herd). This is the densest chiki concentration in the corpus analyzed and matches the 'identity_naming' classifier output. The khipu is recorded in OKR as 'HP051 A' with explicit OKR investigator warnings that (a) pendant 16 is a TRANSVERSAL VERSO variant, (b) 'you cannot read the values because it is not applicable to the usual key of Inca khipus', and that 'HP051 B' (companion khipu) has all S and L knots at the same level. These flags mean HP051 IS ATYPICAL -- it does not follow the standard Locke knot convention, so our normal dictionary lookups require extra caution. No KFG twin resolved (so no Ascher layer). Dating by Mode A: classifier proposes 1505/03/06 CE (C1=80 not-standard but consistent with displaced-Mode; C2=67 as year offset gives 1505). The date is candidate, not confirmed.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-14)
  OKR ID: HP051 A (a paired khipu HP051 B also exists per OKR notes).
  32 cords, all L1 (flat 0-SEUL architecture, depth L1).
  10 clusters.
  4 colors: W(24, 75%), MB(6, 19%), AB(1), R0(1). W-dominant monochromatic register with MB phase punctuation.
  Provenance: UNKNOWN. Museum: Museo Nacional de Arqueologia, Antropologia e Historia del Peru (Pueblo Libre, Lima).
  OKR note HP051 A: 'Pendant 16 is a transversal variant of verso type. Values cannot be read with certainty because it does not follow the usual Inca key.'
  OKR note HP051 B: 'On all pendant cords (except the second), all S and L knots are at basically the same level, so values cannot be read with certainty.'
  Both notes signal ATYPICAL KNOT CONVENTIONS -- Locke positional dating may be unreliable here.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  Quality not fully computed (no KFG twin). Most cords resolved via OKR only.
  v1 -> v3: 0 -> 8 STRING readings (+8), 0 -> 3 distinct vocabulary (chiki, chiqa, tama).
  Old v1 claim: 'Purely numerical register, no text -- ALBA syllabary cannot contribute'. v3 REVERSES this: 8 STRING cords now readable.

ASCHER LAYER
  NOT APPLICABLE. No KFG twin found by the Ascher parser (logged: 'no KFG twin found for HP051 -- skipping layer').
  The atypical knot-level convention noted by OKR investigators likely explains why KFG did not catalog this khipu in the sum-registry -- standard Inca-key sum extraction does not apply.
  Consequence: no cross-verification of Locke values via Ascher constraints. All readings rely solely on the merged OKR knot sequence.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  Layout (p1-p32):
    p1 MB = 80  (header, medium-brown)
    p2 AB = 67  (secondary header, alpaca-brown)
    p3-p11 W: small numbers 17/14/3/6/4/5/14/3/10 (observation block)
    p12 R0 = 2 (anomalous red-orange, possible flag)
    p13 W empty
    p14-p18 W: FIVE CONSECUTIVE chiki on white
    p19 W = 12, p20 W = 10
    p21 W: SIXTH chiki
    p22 W = 10, p23-p26 MB: small tens (10, 20, 6, 6)
    p27 MB chiqa (verification marker)
    p28 W tama (group / herd)
    p29-p32 W: empty tail
  The document has a DENSE chiki-cluster in the middle (p14-p21) bracketed by numerical headers (p1-p12) and a closing verification pair (p27-p28) before the empty tail. The chiki cluster plus chiqa + tama = identity-naming with verification closure.

VOCABULARY (3 words, 8 occurrences, v1-syllabic on merged knots, 100% dict hits)
  chiki x6 (reflexive 'self' / 'same' / possibly 'ditto' marker). DOMINANT -- this is the highest chiki count of any analyzed khipu.
  chiqa x1 (verification / truth). Closing seal.
  tama x1 (group / herd / flock). Closing category marker.
  Signature check:
    - Identity/census: chiki x6 + tama = group of individuals. STRONG match to identity_naming.
    - Juridical: no violence words (no taka, naqa, sipi, maqa, kata, maka, tipi).
    - Astronomical: no kaki, mama, kama, maqa.
    - Cadastral: no qaqa, kaqa, taqa, paqa, siqa, waqa.
  Pure identity-census signature.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p14-p18 W chiki chiki chiki chiki chiki (5 consecutive)
  p21 W chiki (6th)
  p27 MB chiqa (verification)
  p28 W tama (group category)

COLOR ROLE MAP
  W (24, 75%) = PRIMARY data + ALL identity/census words.
  MB (6, 19%) = PHASE MARKERS + closing verification (chiqa).
  AB (1) = secondary header (p2 = 67).
  R0 (1) = ANOMALOUS flag cord p12 (value 2). The red-orange color is rare; the '2' on p12 is small and may be a link or pointer.

DATING ATTEMPT
  Classifier proposes 1505/03/06 via Mode A with C1=80, C2=67, C3 unclear. But Mode A requires C1 non-round 13-100. C1=80 is ROUND, which fails the anti-tribute filter. So Mode A proper fails.
  Alternative Mode C reading: if C1=80 is a flag/category code (round value), then year on C2 = 67. 1438+67 = 1505. But Mode C expects C1 to be <= 12 or STRING -- 80 does not qualify. Formally, neither Mode A nor Mode C fits strictly.
  Honest verdict: DATING UNCERTAIN. The classifier's 1505 is a loose interpretation. The khipu's atypical knot convention (flagged by OKR) also weakens Locke-based dating.
  Contextual fallback: Mid-Imperial Inca period (1451-1532 CE) is likely, consistent with chiki-cluster identity conventions attested in UR055 (succession oracle) and AS014 (nomination ceremony).

HYPOTHESIS
  HP051 is a SMALL IDENTITY REGISTER -- possibly a census of a single ayllu household or a list of mit'a workers. The six chiki markers on adjacent white cords enumerate six individuals (or six 'same'-iterations of a single individual's records). The closing pair chiqa + tama ('verified / group') signals that this is a COMPLETED, VERIFIED group-record. Atypical knot conventions suggest the scribe used a LOCAL or SPECIALIZED encoding -- perhaps coastal (non-Cuzco) or a specific institution that did not fully align with the imperial Inca administrative key. The small size (32 cords), lack of Ascher layer, and presentation as 'HP051 A + HP051 B' paired bundle all point to a LOCAL, small-scale administrative function rather than an imperial-grade document.

THE STORY
  Six selves. That is the core of HP051 -- six adjacent cords on white cotton, each reading chiki, 'self', in the middle of a thirty-two-cord document. Five of them in a row at cords fourteen through eighteen, then one more at twenty-one, separated by two small numerical cords that look like transitions or counts.

  The opening of the document is two large numbers on colored cords: eighty on medium-brown at p1, sixty-seven on alpaca-brown at p2. Perhaps a year-and-day header (our classifier speculatively proposes 1505, the Inca Wayna Qhapaq era), perhaps two category headers for two types of tribute unit, perhaps a heading enumeration of a larger population to which the six selves belong.

  Then nine cords of small counts on white -- seventeen, fourteen, three, six, four, five, fourteen, three, ten. Sub-numbers attached to whatever the opening two cords declared. Then at cord twelve a single red-orange cord carrying the value two. The red-orange is almost unique in this document -- a single flag, perhaps a pointer to another khipu.

  And then the chiki cluster begins. Five consecutive white cords: chiki, chiki, chiki, chiki, chiki. The reflexive word in Inca Quechua, meaning 'self' or functioning as a ditto-mark for 'same as above'. Five individuals, or five iterations of a single individual's declaration. At cord twenty-one, a sixth chiki, after a brief numerical break.

  At cord twenty-seven on medium-brown the scribe writes chiqa: verified. At cord twenty-eight on white, tama: group, herd, flock. The six selves are grouped together and their record is certified true.

  The last four cords are empty -- a ritual closure, a blank tail to signal 'document ends here'.

  The investigators who first catalogued HP051 noted that the knots do not follow the usual Inca convention: on the paired khipu HP051 B, all knots are at the same level, which normally encodes different digit places. Someone wrote this register using a local, non-standard encoding -- or a specialized convention that the Inca hierarchy had not imposed on this community. Whatever the language of its numbers, its WORDS -- six selves, one verified, one group -- read in 2026 as clearly as when they were knotted five centuries ago.

SECTION-BY-SECTION READING
  Opening header (p1-p2): large values 80 and 67 on colored cords. Date candidate 1505 (Mode A uncertain).
  Numerical block (p3-p11): nine small values on white. Possibly sub-totals for enumerated items.
  Anomaly cord (p12 R0=2): red-orange flag, possible cross-reference.
  Chiki cluster (p14-p18, p21): SIX reflexive markers -- identity enumeration.
  Closing mini-block (p22-p26 MB): small tens (10, 10, 20, 6, 6).
  Verification seal (p27 MB chiqa + p28 W tama): verified-group closure.
  Empty tail (p29-p32): end-of-document signal.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  Small identity/census khipus are attested in the OKR but are underrepresented in the published literature. The chiki-cluster convention (multiple adjacent 'self' markers) is seen in AS014 (British Museum nomination ceremony, 3 named candidates) and UR055 (succession oracle) but at lower density. HP051's 6 chiki is the densest in the analyzed set.
  Colonial sources (Murua 1590, Cobo 1653) describe local 'khipukamayuq kipan' at the ayllu level -- small personal/household records kept by the local leader. HP051 fits this profile: small, atypical knot convention (local not imperial), personal-scale vocabulary.
  The atypical knot-level convention noted by OKR investigators is ethnohistorically interesting: Inca administration standardized knot conventions at the imperial level, but local/pre-Inca communities retained idiosyncratic encodings. HP051 may be a LATE-IMPERIAL or EARLY-COLONIAL local document that did not fully conform to the imperial standard.
  Provenance unknown is unfortunate; if the Lima museum (MNAAHP) has accession records, those might identify the collection origin.

CROSS-CORPUS
  AS014 (British Museum nomination ceremony) -- 3 named candidates (Chima, Kataki, Wati), BG=name color. CLOSEST TYPE PARALLEL for identity-naming but with BG color convention vs HP051's white-dominant.
  UR055 (succession oracle) -- ORACLE with reflexive -wa suffix, 180 cords. Genre parallel (identity + verification) but much larger.
  HP051 B (paired khipu per OKR) -- physical companion, similarly atypical. Should be read together.
  No Incahuasi parallel -- HP051 is NOT part of the Incahuasi administrative corpus.

KEY PASSAGES
  p1 MB = 80, p2 AB = 67: header pair, possible date encoding.
  p12 R0 = 2: ANOMALOUS red-orange cord, unique flag in the document.
  p14-p18 W chiki chiki chiki chiki chiki: FIVE CONSECUTIVE identity markers.
  p21 W chiki: sixth identity marker.
  p27 MB chiqa + p28 W tama: VERIFICATION + GROUP closure.
  p29-p32 W empty: ritual end-of-document.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  OKR atypical-knot warning: reproducible from cord_notes.
  Chiki x6 dominance: reproducible from v3 output (this read).
  Paired bundle HP051 A + HP051 B: museum inspection.
  No KFG twin: reproducible from MergedCorpus query (no sum registry).
  Dating 1505 candidate: requires manual verification of Mode A/C rules; honest assessment is UNCERTAIN.

CONFIDENCE
  Word reading: HIGH (8/8 dict-confirmed at 100%).
  Document type: MEDIUM-HIGH (identity_naming signature is clear: chiki x6 + chiqa + tama). Small sample size (only 3 unique words) limits confidence.
  Narrative: MEDIUM (internally coherent identity-census reading; atypical knot convention limits inference).
  Data integrity: MEDIUM (no Ascher layer; knot-level ambiguity flagged by OKR).
  Dating: LOW-MEDIUM (1505 candidate via loose Mode A).

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  - 'Purely numerical register, no text, ALBA syllabary cannot contribute' -- REVERSED: 8 STRING readings, 3 distinct words, identity-naming classification.
  - 'Total value 299' -- partial (many L? cords prevent full total; chiki cords are STRING, not summable).
  - Confidence 'low' -- RAISED to medium (HIGH for word reading, LOW for dating).
  - Dating 1505 kept as CANDIDATE with honest uncertainty flag.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 32 cords, 0-SEUL, W-dominant (confirmed).
  - Provenance unknown, MNAAHP Lima (confirmed).
  - Atypical small khipu (confirmed and now contextualized via OKR knot-convention note).
references: Sivan, J. (2026). Deep read HP051 v3 -- contributions/validated/HP051.json (this record).

OKR cord-note on HP051 B: 'On all pendant cords (except the second), all S and L knots are at basically the same level.'
Khipu Field Guide catalog: not indexed (no KFG twin resolved).

Cobo, B. (1653). Historia del Nuevo Mundo -- personal / household record conventions.
